explanation:
Given, $ x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0 $

$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x} \\ \Rightarrow & (x \sqrt{1+y})^{2}=(-y \sqrt{1+x})^{2} \end{array} $

[squaring both sides]

$ \begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & x^{2}(1+y)=y^{2}(1+x) \\ \Rightarrow & x^{2}-y^{2}=y^{2} x-x^{2} y \\ \Rightarrow & (x-y)(x+y)=x y(y-x) \\ \Rightarrow & x+y=-x y \\ \Rightarrow & x+y+x y=0 \\ \Rightarrow & y=\frac{-x}{1+x}=-1+\frac{1}{1+x} \end{array} $

Differentiating w.r.t, $ x $

$ \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{(1+x)^{2}} $

Given, $$y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - x} \over {1 + x}}} } \right)$$

Let $$x = \cos 2\theta \Rightarrow y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over {1 + \cos 2\theta }}} } \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2{{\cos }^2}\theta }}} } \right) \Rightarrow y = \sin [2{\tan ^{ - 1}}(\tan \theta )]$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \sin 2\theta \Rightarrow y = \sqrt {1 - {{\cos }^2}2\theta } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} $$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}( - 2x) = - {x \over {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}$$

content: Let f(x) be a polynomial function of second degree. If f(1) = f($$-$$1) and a, b, c are in AP, then f'(a), f'(b) and f'(c) are in.
explanation:
Let f(x) = Ax2 + Bx+ C

$$\therefore$$ f(1) = A + B + C

and f($$-$$1) = A $$-$$ B + C

$$\because$$ f(1) = f($$-$$1)

$$\Rightarrow$$ A + B + C = A $$-$$ B + C

$$\Rightarrow$$ 2B = 0 $$\Rightarrow$$ B = 0

$$\therefore$$ f(x) = Ax2 + C

$$\Rightarrow$$ f'(x) = 2Ax

$$\Rightarrow$$ f'(a) = 2Aa

f'(b) = 2Ab and f'(c) = 2Ac

Also, a, b, c are in AP

So, 2Aa, 2Ab and 2Ac are in AP.

$$\Rightarrow$$ f'(a), f'(b) and f'(c) are also in AP.

content: Given that $$f(x) = 2{x^3} + {x^4} + \log x$$ and assuming g to be the inverse function of f, compute the value of g'(3).
